tweets.text:
Kareem believes most founders and creatives create from a place of lack, a need to prove something or to be loved.

He creates from a place of wholeness instead. We coined a term for it: "post-lack."

"VCs have this common trope, that they invest in someone who has a chip on their shoulder, or some kind of lack they're trying to fulfill. 

It's common across creative work. 

The trope is you're depressed or anxious, and you're trying to fill that, and you're actually afraid to lose the depression or anxiety because you feel like you'll be less productive or creative.

I've had friends say, "I don't care if you succeed or fail, I love you." 

And I was like, "Wow, that's actually helpful." I didn't think it would be, but it's feeding some part of my desire must be fueled by that, but I can get that right now.  

And now I can start to take real risks where I'm thinking about the business and not trying to fill this like particular need.

I had this moment where I'm not going to create from a place of lack anymore. I went around telling people, I have everything I need. 

And from there you can still create.

People are tremendously misunderstanding this. You can create from a place of wholeness. 

And the more whole you can be, the more risks you can take, and the more courage you'll have, because you have nothing to lose."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
One of the most unique conversations I've had in a while.

Kareem Amin is co-founder and CEO of Clay, which has grown from $1M to $100M ARR in the last two years and is valued at $5B.

He is also one of the most original and deep thinkers I've met, which has led him to build the https://t.co/hzrAYhGvop

tweets.text:
Kareem believes capitalism rewards risk more than hard work or skill. 

He has a really unique way of defining risk and explains why most people never actually take any:

"Capitalism rewards risk more than anything else. 

A lot of people think it's a meritocracy, but there are a lot of people working really hard and not making a lot of money. 

It doesn't just reward skill either. 

You can be eating a sword and not making as much as someone doing a YouTube video.

A lot of people think they're taking risks, but aren't taking real risks. 

I talked to one founder who went to a good school, then YC, and he was telling me about his company. 

He said, 'We serve sales, and we serve recruiting.' 

I said, 'You have to pick one.' 

That's where you're actually providing value –– in committing yourself to serving some group of people in some specific way.

My definition of risk is you need to genuinely not know what's going to happen, so you're going to discover something new. And it needs to be coupled with a high potential for shame. 

That's when you can take real risk.

When you couple risk and courage, the next part is commitment. You actually need to follow through to see what ends up happening.

One of the most unique conversations I've had in a while.

Kareem Amin is co-founder and CEO of Clay, which has grown from $1M to $100M ARR in the last two years and is valued at $5B.

He is also one of the most original and deep thinkers I've met, which has led him to build the https://t.co/hzrAYhGvop
tweets.full_text:
Kareem believes capitalism rewards risk more than hard work or skill. 

He has a really unique way of defining risk and explains why most people never actually take any:

"Capitalism rewards risk more than anything else. 

A lot of people think it's a meritocracy, but there are a lot of people working really hard and not making a lot of money. 

It doesn't just reward skill either. 

You can be eating a sword and not making as much as someone doing a YouTube video.

A lot of people think they're taking risks, but aren't taking real risks. 

I talked to one founder who went to a good school, then YC, and he was telling me about his company. 

He said, 'We serve sales, and we serve recruiting.' 

I said, 'You have to pick one.' 

That's where you're actually providing value –– in committing yourself to serving some group of people in some specific way.

My definition of risk is you need to genuinely not know what's going to happen, so you're going to discover something new. And it needs to be coupled with a high potential for shame. 

That's when you can take real risk.

When you couple risk and courage, the next part is commitment. You actually need to follow through to see what ends up happening. 

You can't just quit in the beginning."

tweets.text:
Our report "A Framework For Crypto Neobanks" is now live!

Stablecoin payment infrastructure is inevitable but the crypto companies built for it may not be.

The category has fragmented into five distinct models that each occupy a different layer of the stack. The survivors will either own infrastructure, own distribution, or specialize in markets traditional banks fail to serve.

Rain and RedotPay are early proof of both paths. Rain holds a Visa principal membership and sells issuance infrastructure to other crypto apps. RedotPay focuses on emerging markets where dollar access is the actual product.

Across 190+ tracked crypto card programs, Rain and RedotPay account for roughly 90% of observed onchain volume while everyone else is fighting over the remainder.

Then there's the settlement layer. Visa's stablecoin settlement pilot has hit a $7B annualized run rate across nine chains. Mastercard is moving in the same direction with expanded stablecoin settlement capabilities. The incumbents are extending downward into it before a parallel rail can form outside their networks.

The most likely path is not a clean victory for either side. Incumbents will adopt stablecoin rails. A select few crypto companies will still command the volume. The technology can win while most of the companies fall behind.

tweets.text:
Just some random thoughts, I do think AI is the most disruptive technology in human history. 

To the level of agricultural or industrial revolution.

Since Anthropic, OpenAi, XAI, and others are racing to build superintelligence. 

The amount of economic impact can't be measured if AI helps find cures for cancer or accelerates discovery for Quantum Computing. 

Or if AI end up displacing the workforce, which increases profitability for companies. 

The US Gov has every incentive to keep the buildout going too, as the implications from Warfare, Cybersecurity, is also immeasurable if China takes the lead. 

So there's likely to be incentives and subsidies to win, even if there's not enough profit derived LLM training/inference. 

As for sustainability, when you look upstream, $GOOGL is able to fund it majorly with their own cashflow, same with $AMZN, $MSFT. 

More lukewarm on $META. Very iffy about $ORCL. 

But I do see some bubbles forming around debt interest like $CRWV.

Maybe circular valuations that's happening with OpenAI backlog agreements or $NVDA / $AMD agreements with Neoclouds to buy their GPUs.

But as seen with $MSFT and having OpenAI be a major part of the backlog, it did correct off the information, so "bubbles" like that do pop despite the overall markets increasing.

Definitely don't see a bubble in upstream semiconductors from $LITE to Sk Hynix though since the amount of profit they get from the buildout would likely be insane to make up for capex decreasing. 

OpenAI was actually my biggest fear from contagion, eg. $CRWV, $CBRS and others, but they just raised a lot.

So think it will be fine for another 1 1/2 years of capex, especially if they IPO this year. 

I also don't think we'll get massive Fed tightening despite "predictions" since this will trigger a contagion since many of these players rely heavily on debt. 

And although the Fed is independent, don't think Trump would have supported someone who is against his administration goals.  

As for semiconductor valuations going up every day like $AMD or $MU, there's probably going to be some corrections here and there. Everything going up together is kinda unhealthy. 

Can't time the capex peak but just from $AVGO and other projections, it just keeps accelerating exponentially into 2028. 

Especially as everyone is starting to sign multi year agreements as well. 

OpenAI contagion / hyperscaler capex decreasing / fed tightening was what I'm looking out for, and no blaring signs of any of those yet. 

So I think the music will keep playing for this year at the bare minimum.

tweets.text:
$VELVET went up ~20x and then gave almost all of it back in a single day.

Definitely sus. While the price action is something I avoid, I keep reminding myself is the token's chart and the actual product are two totally different things. 

So I played around with the @Velvet_Capital terminal to check out the product.

It's a whole trading terminal, spot and perps and yield. The most useful area for me is the Trenches tab. 

It sorts new launches by stage and lets you filter by liquidity and holders, so you can just glance and see what's catching on vs what already ran. Saves me bouncing between five windows like I usually do.

→ Robot "brains" that learn how to pour water without spilling it, the same way an LLM learns language

→ The shift in healthcare from chemical whack-a-mole to bio cures

→ Why every family should hold six months of Bitcoin, and why a treasurer or central bank without it is being irresponsible

→ The best advice I've ever heard anyone give an entrepreneur

→ And of course… my favorite failures of my career

Thank you Nicole and the Market Overtime team for having me.

tweets.text:
This is a STRF-like product, not a STRC-like product. It means that the yield is fixed at 9.5% (on a $100 price), but the price can float, the market forces will define it.

The problem is that we don't know how levered Tom Lee wants its structure to be, because that's what should define the effective yield on $BMNP:
The lower the leverage of BMNR, the higher the price of BMNP should be, hence the lower the yield. (BMNP at $190 is a 5% effective yield, BMNP at $95 is a 10% effective yield)

Currently, the leverage of BMNR is very low, so if Tom Lee doesn't ATM new BMNP shares, its price should easily be able to get to $100 and even more, because it's really not far from a risk-free product.

But the high likelihood that Tom Lee would want to issue more BMNP if price goes up a lot does obviously cap it, probably not far from $100 because it's a Schelling point.

thread_tweets.text:
By the way, that's something that people never talk about when they criticize MSTR, and yet it's crucial: theoretically, MSTR could simply exist with one common stock (MSTR) + one STRF pref share, and limit the leverage at like 10% or even less.

In that situation, the leverage of MSTR would be low, so STRF should trade at a high price (and low effective yield), so MSTR would be able to "borrow USD at a very low cost" (maybe like 6%-7%) to lever up and buy BTC, and it would be almost certain to outperform BTC, but at the cost of not being able to outperform by a wide margin (Because of low 1.1x leverage vs BTC)...

The higher leverage, the bigger the outperformance vs BTC, but also the lower the likelihood of actually outperforming it. Because when the leverage is very high, a treasury company becomes much more fragile towards volatility, and is taking some risk on the downside.

All of that is very logical, but is literally never talked about on CT: any person who criticizes MSTR should outline what leverage ratio seems reasonable for a BTC treasury company, assuming they expect BTC to have a positive long-term CAGR.
